title: RAG Book
text:
Preface
Retrieval-Augmented Generation, usually shortened to RAG, is one of the most useful ways to build applications with large language models. Instead of asking a model to answer from pretraining alone, a RAG system looks up relevant information at runtime and asks the model to answer with that evidence in view.
That sounds simple, but in practice RAG is not one trick. It is a pipeline. Documents have to be prepared. Chunks have to be indexed. Retrieval has to bring back the right evidence. Prompts have to make the model use that evidence well. And the whole system has to be evaluated so we can tell whether it is actually helping.
This book is written for readers who want that pipeline explained in a practical way. The goal is not to show off every tool in the ecosystem. The goal is to make the moving parts clear enough that you can understand them, debug them, and build with them.

title: RAG Book
RAG Book
RAG Book
text:
How the Book Is Organized
The book is organized into seven main chapters:

Introduction to RAG explains what RAG is, what problem it solves, and where its limits begin.
Data Preparation shows how raw source material becomes retrieval-ready chunks.
Index Construction and Optimization explains how those chunks become searchable.
Retrieval Optimization focuses on how to find the right evidence more reliably.
Generation Integration shows how retrieved evidence becomes a grounded answer.
RAG System Evaluation explains how to tell which part of the system is working and which part is not.
Advanced RAG Architectures asks when a baseline pipeline is no longer enough and what comes next.

title: RAG Book
text:
A Practical Reading Strategy
If you are new to RAG, start with Chapters 1 through 3 to build a strong base. If you already have a prototype and need better results, Chapters 4 through 6 will likely be the highest leverage. If you are designing longer-term platform capabilities, Chapter 7 will help you think beyond the default vector-search pattern.
Throughout the book, the emphasis is on decisions. A RAG system is rarely made good by one clever trick. It becomes good when each layer is good enough for the use case, and when the handoff between layers is clear.

title: RAG Book
text:
Final Note Before We Begin
RAG is sometimes marketed as an easy way to bolt external knowledge onto a model. In reality, it is closer to information retrieval, data preparation, prompt design, and evaluation working together. That complexity is exactly why it is worth studying carefully. When the pieces align, RAG can be one of the most effective ways to build reliable LLM applications.
The next chapter starts with the most basic question in the whole subject: what exactly is RAG, and why do people use it in the first place?

title: 1  Introduction to RAG
text:
1.1 What Is Retrieval-Augmented Generation
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an application pattern in which a language model receives external information at inference time and uses that information to produce an answer. Rather than relying only on parameters learned during pretraining, the model is given retrieved evidence that is relevant to the current query.
At a high level, a RAG system has three stages: retrieve, augment, generate. A user question triggers retrieval, useful passages or records are fetched from a knowledge source, and the model answers with that evidence in view. The key benefit is that the knowledge source can be updated independently of the model itself.
In plainer language, RAG is a way of letting the model look things up before it answers.
That matters because many useful questions depend on information that is:

title: 1  Introduction to RAG
1  Introduction to RAG
text:
1.3 Evolution of RAG Techniques
Early RAG implementations were mostly “semantic search plus prompt stuffing.” They embedded chunks, retrieved the top k, and placed them into a template. That pattern still works for many small systems, but the field has evolved rapidly.
Modern systems often include hybrid retrieval, metadata filtering, multi-stage ranking, context compression, tool use, query routing, and domain-specific structured retrieval. The trend is clear: the retrieval stack is becoming more adaptive, and the generation stack is becoming more constrained and instrumented.

title: 1  Introduction to RAG
text:
1.4 RAG vs. Fine-Tuning
RAG and fine-tuning solve different problems.
RAG is strongest when the challenge is access to external knowledge: product catalogs, internal documentation, support articles, contracts, policies, and rapidly changing facts. Fine-tuning is strongest when the challenge is behavior: tone, style, task formatting, response patterns, or domain-specific output conventions.
In practice, many mature systems combine the two. A model may be tuned to follow a task reliably while RAG provides fresh or private facts. The common mistake is assuming one replaces the other in all cases.
If you want a short version:

RAG is usually about giving the model better knowledge at answer time;
fine-tuning is usually about changing how the model behaves.

title: 1  Introduction to RAG
section: 1.5 When to Use RAG: Use Cases and Risk Levels

title: 1  Introduction to RAG
text:
1.7 Getting Started with RAG Systems
The best way to begin is not with the fanciest architecture. Start with a narrow use case, a clear corpus, and a measurable task. Build a baseline pipeline:

Ingest a bounded set of documents.
Normalize and chunk them.
Embed and index the chunks.
Retrieve top results for a test query set.
Prompt the model with retrieved evidence.
Evaluate answer quality and failure modes.

This baseline reveals where the real bottleneck lives. For many teams, the first bottleneck is not model choice but document quality and query coverage.

title: 1  Introduction to RAG
1  Introduction to RAG
section: 1.8 Is RAG Dead? Debates and Emerging Alternatives
text:
1.8 Is RAG Dead? Debates and Emerging Alternatives
Claims that “RAG is dead” usually reflect a narrower point: long-context models, tool use, and stronger base models reduce the need for naive retrieval pipelines. They do not eliminate the need for retrieval itself.
In reality, the question is shifting from “Should we use RAG?” to “What retrieval strategy matches this task?” Sometimes the answer is classic vector search. Sometimes it is SQL generation, graph traversal, API calls, or an orchestrated combination of them.

title: 1  Introduction to RAG
text:
1.8 A Minimal RAG Implementation
The smallest useful RAG system can be expressed in pseudocode:
documents -> clean -> chunk -> embed -> index
user query -> embed -> retrieve top_k chunks
top_k chunks + instructions + question -> language model
language model -> answer with citations or grounded summary
Frameworks such as LangChain and LlamaIndex can accelerate assembly, while local model runners such as Ollama can help with experimentation. Still, the framework is not the system. Good results come from good data, good retrieval, and clear evaluation, not only from wiring together abstractions.

1.8.1 A Configurable Demo with Ollama
To make the architecture concrete, this book uses an Ollama-based setup and turns the book itself into the knowledge base. The current project supports both local models and Ollama cloud models for generation, while the embedding side is typically built with a local embedding model such as bge-m3:latest.

default local generator model: qwen3.5:2b
default embedding model: bge-m3:latest
optional generator choice: selectable local or Ollama cloud model
ingestion script: code/cli/ingest_book.py
indexing script: code/cli/build_book_index.py
question-answering script: code/cli/ask_book.py

The workflow is intentionally practical:

read the .qmd chapters listed in _quarto.yml;
turn them into chunks with source-path metadata;
embed those chunks with a selected embedding model, usually bge-m3:latest;
store the result as a local JSON index;
answer questions against the book with a selected Ollama chat model.



1.8.2 Running the Example
If you want to use local models, pull them first:
ollama pull qwen3.5:2b
ollama pull bge-m3:latest
If you want to use Ollama cloud models, add OLLAMA_API_KEY to the project’s .env file.
Then build and query the book index. A local default path looks like this:
python3 code/cli/ingest_book.py
python3 code/cli/build_book_index.py --embed-model bge-m3:latest
python3 code/cli/ask_book.py --question "What does the book say about hybrid retrieval?" --chat-model qwen3.5:2b --embed-model bge-m3:latest
In the Streamlit interface, the same pipeline is exposed through model selectors in the sidebar, so the reader can switch chat models without editing code.


1.8.3 Core Code Path
The key ingestion step turns book chapters into reusable chunks:
def build_corpus() -> list[dict[str, str]]:
    corpus: list[dict[str, str]] = []
    for document in load_book_documents():
        corpus.extend(chunk_document(document["path"], document["text"]))
    return corpus
The answering step then retrieves matching chunks from the built index and passes them to the selected generator. This keeps the example tied to a real artifact: if the chapter text changes, the corpus and answers can change too.


1.8.4 Why This Example Matters
A project like this is useful for two reasons. First, it gives the reader a baseline that can run entirely on local models, while still allowing a faster or stronger cloud chat model when desired. Second, it answers a real question: how do we build a RAG system over a body of source material that we actually care about?
In a real project, the next upgrades would usually be better chunking, metadata-aware filtering, a persistent vector store, and stronger evaluation. Those improvements make more sense once a baseline exists and can already answer questions over the full book.

title: 2  Data Preparation
text:
2.1 The Role of Data in RAG Systems
Data preparation is the foundation of the entire pipeline. Retrieval systems cannot surface information that was never ingested, cannot recover semantics lost during parsing, and cannot rank chunks well if the text boundaries are broken.
In many failing RAG systems, retrieval is blamed first. Yet the underlying issue is often incomplete coverage, duplicated content, malformed parsing, or weak metadata. Strong data preparation reduces all of those risks.
In this project, data preparation is not an abstract preprocessing phase. It is the part of the system that turns the book’s .qmd chapters into a retrieval corpus that can later be embedded, searched, and used as evidence for grounded answers. If this step is weak, every downstream step inherits the weakness.
Another way to say this is simple: data preparation decides what the system is able to know later. If important text never enters the corpus, retrieval cannot find it. If the text enters in a broken form, retrieval may find the wrong thing. If the chunk is messy, the model may answer from partial or confusing evidence.

title: 2  Data Preparation
text:
2.4 Document Ingestion and Parsing
Ingestion is the process of collecting raw material from source systems such as PDFs, HTML pages, markdown files, databases, wikis, tickets, and cloud storage. Parsing then converts those materials into a structured internal representation.
The central requirement is fidelity. Titles, sections, tables, lists, code blocks, dates, and authorship metadata often carry critical meaning. If the parser collapses structure too aggressively, downstream retrieval quality declines.
In the current repository, ingestion starts from the Quarto book configuration. The system reads _quarto.yml, finds the listed chapter files, and then loads their contents as documents:
def parse_quarto_chapters() -> list[Path]:
    config_path = PROJECT_DIR / "_quarto.yml"
    lines = config_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines()
    chapters: list[Path] = []
    in_chapters = False
    for raw_line in lines:
        line = raw_line.rstrip()
        stripped = line.strip()
        if stripped == "chapters:":
            in_chapters = True
            continue
        if in_chapters:
            if re.match(r"^[A-Za-z]", line):
                break
            match = re.match(r"^\s*-\s+(.+\.qmd)\s*$", line)
            if match:
                path = PROJECT_DIR / match.group(1)
                if path.name != "references.qmd":
                    chapters.append(path)
    return chapters
This is a simple example, but it illustrates an important principle: ingestion should preserve document boundaries and document identity. The retriever later benefits from knowing not only the text itself, but also where that text came from.

2.4.1 Case Study: Technical Chapters as Retrieval Documents
Suppose the source material is a chapter with headings, paragraphs, and code examples:
## Hybrid Retrieval

Hybrid retrieval combines dense and sparse methods.

Dense retrieval helps capture semantic similarity.

Sparse retrieval helps preserve exact keyword matching.
If this content is parsed poorly, several things can go wrong:

the heading may be dropped;
the paragraphs may be merged into one undifferentiated block;
the text may later be split in a place that separates the concept from its explanation.

In retrieval terms, that means a user may retrieve only a partial definition, only a supporting detail, or a chunk with weak contextual labeling. The parser’s job is therefore not merely to read bytes. It is to preserve enough structure for later retrieval to make sense.

title: 2  Data Preparation
2  Data Preparation
text:
2.6 Structured and Unstructured Data Pipelines
RAG often begins with unstructured text, but many valuable systems depend on structured data too. Product specs, transaction records, support metrics, and knowledge graphs may all contribute useful evidence.
Unstructured pipelines usually optimize for semantic retrieval. Structured pipelines optimize for precise filtering, joins, and field-level semantics. Mature systems often combine both and route queries differently depending on the user intent.
For example, a chapter in this book is mostly unstructured text. A product catalog is more structured. An FAQ page sits somewhere in between. That difference matters because not every source should be chunked in the same way. A free-form article often benefits from paragraph-aware chunking. An FAQ often works better if one question and one answer stay together as one chunk.

title: 2  Data Preparation
text:
2.7 Text Cleaning and Normalization
Cleaning should be conservative. Remove obvious noise such as duplicated headers, invisible characters, broken whitespace, or irrelevant boilerplate. Keep information that affects meaning, such as section labels, table headings, bullet relationships, and legal disclaimers.
Normalization practices commonly include:

standardizing whitespace and line breaks;
preserving heading hierarchy;
converting lists into readable plain text;
keeping source IDs and timestamps;
attaching provenance metadata to each chunk.

The right question is always: what context will the retriever and model need later?
That question sounds basic, but it prevents a common mistake. Many people clean text to make it look tidy. What matters is not whether the text looks tidy. What matters is whether the text still contains the clues needed to answer a future question.
The current project uses a lightweight cleaning function before chunking:
def strip_quarto_noise(text: str) -> str:
    text = re.sub(r"^---\n.*?\n---\n", "", text, flags=re.DOTALL)
    text = re.sub(r"```.*?```", "", text, flags=re.DOTALL)
    text = re.sub(r"\{\.unnumbered\}", "", text)
    return text
This code removes front matter, fenced code blocks, and a Quarto-specific class marker. The example is useful because it shows both the value and the risk of cleaning. Removing noise can improve retrieval focus, but over-cleaning can also discard information that a user might reasonably ask about.

2.7.1 Case Study: Cleaning Too Little vs. Cleaning Too Much
Consider a chapter that begins with front matter and formatting metadata:
---
title: "Data Preparation"
---

# Data Preparation {.unnumbered}

Chunking is one of the highest-leverage decisions in RAG.
If the system leaves all of that text untouched, retrieval may surface chunks dominated by formatting artifacts rather than substantive content. A user asking about chunking does not need YAML headers or Quarto markers.
But cleaning can also go too far. Imagine a policy document like this:
Access levels:
- Admin: full write access
- Editor: content update only
- Viewer: read only
If cleaning removes the heading or flattens the list into unclear prose, the retriever may lose exactly the distinctions the user cares about. The lesson is simple: cleaning should remove noise, not structure.

title: 2  Data Preparation
2  Data Preparation
text:
2.10 Fixed-Size Chunking
Fixed-size chunking is the simplest baseline: split text into windows of n tokens or characters, optionally with overlap. It is easy to implement and often surprisingly effective for homogeneous documents.
Its weakness is semantic blindness. Paragraph boundaries, headings, and tables are ignored unless preserved separately. Fixed-size chunking is best treated as a baseline, not a universal answer.

title: 2  Data Preparation
text:
2.11 Recursive Chunking Strategies
Recursive chunking improves on fixed-size chunking by attempting larger structural boundaries first, then falling back to smaller ones. For example, a pipeline may split by heading, then paragraph, then sentence if necessary.
This strategy tends to preserve coherence better while still keeping chunk lengths within target ranges. It is a strong general-purpose default for many text corpora.
If fixed-size chunking asks “How many characters should I cut?”, recursive chunking asks “Can I split this at a natural boundary first?” That is usually a better question.

title: 2  Data Preparation
text:
2.12 Semantic Chunking
Semantic chunking tries to split text where the topic changes rather than where the character count ends. This can improve retrieval quality when documents contain long sections that drift across subtopics.
The tradeoff is complexity. Semantic chunking can be slower, harder to debug, and sensitive to the segmentation model. It works best when the corpus is dense and concept-heavy enough to justify the extra effort.
This sounds attractive, but it is not always the right first step. If the team still does not understand its documents, metadata, or evaluation set, semantic chunking can add complexity before the basics are under control.

title: 2  Data Preparation
text:
2.13 Structure-Aware Chunking
Structure-aware chunking uses document features such as headings, captions, table boundaries, slide sections, or code blocks. This is often the best choice for technical documentation, legal texts, and academic materials where structure conveys meaning.
Examples include:

keeping one FAQ item per chunk;
preserving an entire table with its caption and notes;
keeping code examples together with their explanations;
splitting policies by numbered clause.


2.13.1 Case Study: A Bad Chunk and a Better Chunk
Suppose a chapter contains this short section:
## RAG vs. Fine-Tuning

RAG is strongest when the challenge is access to external knowledge.

Fine-tuning is strongest when the challenge is behavior, style, or output format.
A poor fixed split might produce:
Chunk A:
RAG is strongest when the challenge is access to external knowledge. Fine-tuning

Chunk B:
is strongest when the challenge is behavior, style, or output format.
This split damages the comparison itself. A user asking about the difference between RAG and fine-tuning may retrieve only half the contrast.
A better structure-aware chunk would keep the short section intact:
source_path: 01-introduction-to-rag.qmd
section: RAG vs. Fine-Tuning
text:
RAG is strongest when the challenge is access to external knowledge.

Fine-tuning is strongest when the challenge is behavior, style, or output format.
This version is better for retrieval because:

the heading tells the retriever and the user what question the chunk answers;
the comparison stays together instead of being split across chunk boundaries;
the source path makes later attribution straightforward.



2.13.2 Case Study: FAQ-Style Content
Some materials already contain natural chunk boundaries. FAQ documents are a good example:
Q: When should RAG be preferred over fine-tuning?
A: When the main challenge is accessing external or changing knowledge rather than changing model behavior.
If the question and answer are split into different chunks, retrieval may find the wording of the question but miss the answer itself. A better strategy is to keep one FAQ item as one chunk, because the semantic unit is the whole exchange, not the individual lines.
This example shows a broader point: chunking should follow meaning when possible. Uniform chunk sizes are useful, but document semantics matter more.

title: 2  Data Preparation
text:
2.15 Chunking Strategies Across Frameworks
Most frameworks provide utilities for chunking, but the decision logic still belongs to the system designer. The same corpus can yield very different results under different chunk sizes, overlaps, and structure rules.
A practical process is to test chunking on a representative query set, inspect retrieved passages manually, and iterate. Chunking is not a preprocessing footnote. It is a retrieval design choice that strongly shapes everything that follows.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.1 From Chunks to Searchable Units
At the end of Chapter 2, we had a set of chunks that were clean enough and small enough to retrieve. But those chunks are still just text. The system still needs a way to search them well.
That is the job of indexing.
In plain language, indexing means turning chunks into a form that the retrieval step can search quickly and usefully. In this project, that mostly means creating embeddings for each chunk and saving them in an index file.
So the flow now looks like this:
This chapter is about that middle step.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.2 Evolution of Embedding Models
Embedding models have evolved from relatively narrow sentence encoders into stronger multilingual and domain-aware models. Better training data, contrastive objectives, and larger model capacities have all improved retrieval quality.
At the same time, the problem remains use-case dependent. A model that performs well on public benchmarks may still struggle on internal jargon, code snippets, legal clauses, or highly structured records.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.3 Training Principles of Embedding Models
Many modern embedding models are trained with contrastive learning. Positive pairs are pulled together in vector space, while negatives are pushed apart. This teaches the model a geometry of similarity.
The important practical insight is that “similarity” depends on the training objective. Semantic equivalence, topical relatedness, paraphrase behavior, and relevance for retrieval are related but not identical concepts.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.5 Multimodal Embeddings
Not all knowledge is textual. Many real systems need to search over images, slides, screenshots, diagrams, and mixed media. Multimodal embeddings aim to place text and non-text inputs in compatible representational spaces.
This enables cross-modal retrieval, such as finding figures from a textual query or retrieving image-caption pairs that explain a concept.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.6 CLIP and Vision-Language Representations
CLIP is a landmark example of joint text-image representation learning. By aligning images and their textual descriptions, it demonstrated how a shared embedding space can support flexible retrieval across modalities.
The broader lesson is that RAG can operate over any evidence type that can be indexed and retrieved, not just prose paragraphs.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.7 Multimodal Embedding Models in Practice
In practice, multimodal systems need careful document modeling. An image alone may be insufficient; the surrounding caption, section title, page number, and document identity may be equally important retrieval signals.
Good multimodal indexing often combines vector similarity with structural metadata rather than treating the embedding as the only source of truth.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.8 Vector Databases and Similarity Search
Vector databases store embeddings and support nearest-neighbor search. They usually also support metadata filtering, persistence, and operational features such as replication or sharding.
For small experiments, a local index may be enough. For production systems with large corpora, frequent updates, or multi-tenant workloads, operational concerns become central.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.9 Vector Indexing and ANN Search
Approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) indexing makes vector retrieval tractable at scale. Structures such as HNSW, IVF, and PQ trade exactness for speed and memory efficiency.
These choices directly affect recall, latency, and cost. Index tuning is therefore a product decision as much as an engineering one.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.10 Local Vector Stores (FAISS)
FAISS is widely used for local experimentation and efficient similarity search. It is flexible, fast, and ideal for prototyping retrieval behavior before committing to a larger serving stack.
Its limitations are mostly operational. Production deployments often need higher-level concerns such as multi-user isolation, managed persistence, or distributed scaling.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.11 Distributed Vector Databases (Milvus)
Distributed systems such as Milvus are designed for larger-scale workloads, where indexing, serving, and storage need to be managed across nodes. They are useful when datasets are large, update frequency is significant, or operational guarantees matter.
Choosing such a system should be justified by workload needs, not by trend-following alone.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.13 Context Expansion Strategies
Sometimes the top retrieved chunk is too narrow to be useful in isolation. Context expansion methods add neighboring chunks, parent sections, or linked records to reconstruct the local information neighborhood.
This can improve answer quality, especially in documents with strong sequential structure. The cost is extra tokens and possible noise, so it should be evaluated carefully.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.14 Structured Indexing
Not all retrieval targets belong in a vector store alone. Tables, entities, graphs, and canonical records may be better indexed in structured systems alongside vector search.
Structured indexing is often what separates a demo from a robust product. The strongest systems use each storage and retrieval mechanism for the type of knowledge it represents best.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.1 Why Retrieval Still Fails After Indexing
By the end of Chapter 3, the system has chunks, embeddings, and an index. That is enough to start retrieving. But it is not enough to guarantee good retrieval.
This is the next important idea in RAG: having a searchable index does not mean the system will reliably find the best evidence.
A system can still fail in ways like these:
So this chapter is about a more specific question than the last one.
Chapter 3 asked:
Chapter 4 asks:

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.3 Hybrid Retrieval Systems
Hybrid retrieval combines dense and sparse signals. This is often one of the highest-return upgrades for a baseline system because the two methods fail differently.
Dense retrieval broadens semantic coverage; sparse retrieval preserves exact matching. Together, they often improve recall and robustness.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.5 Query Construction
Users do not always phrase questions in ways that match the underlying corpus. Query construction bridges that gap by expanding, reformulating, or decomposing the request into retrieval-friendly forms.
Examples include generating synonyms, extracting entities, or turning a broad question into several narrower subqueries.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.6 Text-to-Metadata Filtering
Metadata filters can sharply improve retrieval precision. If the user asks about a specific product line, date range, region, document type, or policy version, the retriever should use that structure whenever possible.
This reduces search space and often makes the answer more auditable.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.7 Text-to-Cypher Queries
When knowledge is represented as a graph, the retrieval problem changes. Instead of fetching free-text chunks, the system may need to generate a graph query such as Cypher to traverse entities and relationships.
This is useful when the user asks questions about connections, dependencies, ownership, lineage, or multi-hop relationships.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.8 Text-to-SQL Retrieval Systems
For tabular or warehouse-backed data, a natural-language request may be better handled by generating SQL than by retrieving prose. This is especially true for analytical questions, aggregations, or filtered summaries.
Text-to-SQL should still be considered part of retrieval in the broad sense: it is a method for obtaining the evidence needed to answer.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.9 Query Rewriting and Reformulation
Query rewriting can improve difficult searches, especially when user queries are vague, conversational, or underspecified. A system may rewrite a question into a canonical form before retrieval, or generate several alternative search intents in parallel.
The main risk is drift. Rewriting should help recover relevant evidence, not silently change the user’s meaning.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.10 Query Routing and Distribution
Advanced systems do not send every question to the same retriever. They route based on intent, modality, risk level, or source type. One query might go to a vector index, another to SQL, and another to a graph store.
Routing turns retrieval into orchestration. The challenge is deciding when the extra complexity yields enough quality gain.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.12 Re-ranking Methods
Re-rankers apply a stronger relevance model to a small candidate set. Cross-encoders, reranking APIs, or task-specific scoring heuristics can all improve the quality of the final selected context.
They are especially valuable when the first-stage retriever has good recall but mediocre ordering.
In a local-first setup, a useful exercise is to first compare dense, sparse, and hybrid retrieval without a re-ranker. Only after that baseline is understood should a stronger second-stage scorer be added. This keeps the source of gains interpretable.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.13 Context Compression
Even good retrieval can return more text than the generator should receive. Compression methods trim, summarize, or extract the most relevant spans before generation.
Compression can reduce token cost and improve focus, but it must preserve the evidence needed for faithful answers.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.14 Retrieval Correction
No retrieval stack is perfect. Strong systems include correction strategies such as fallback searches, empty-result handling, confidence thresholds, or user clarification loops.
The practical objective is not perfect retrieval. It is graceful behavior when retrieval is uncertain or incomplete.
The local scripts in this book are intentionally transparent, so the reader can inspect the retrieved chapter chunks directly. That manual inspection habit is often the fastest way to diagnose whether a problem comes from chunking, embeddings, lexical mismatch, or the generation prompt.

title: 5  Generation Integration
text:
5.1 Retrieval Is Not the Final Answer
By the end of Chapter 4, the system has selected chunks that look useful. But retrieved evidence is not the same thing as a good answer.
This is where generation begins.
The generator has to do a harder job than it may first appear:
So the main question of this chapter is simple:

title: 5  Generation Integration
5  Generation Integration
text:
5.3 Structured Output Generation
Many RAG systems should not return free-form prose alone. They may need JSON, tables, lists of citations, extracted fields, or machine-actionable results.
Structured output reduces ambiguity, makes downstream integration easier, and can improve evaluation because outputs become easier to compare.

title: 5  Generation Integration
text:
5.4 JSON and Schema-Based Generation
Schema-based generation defines the fields the model must produce and the constraints each field must follow. This is especially useful for extraction, classification, workflow triggering, or UI-backed applications.
When schema validation fails, the system can retry, repair, or fall back. This turns generation into a more dependable software component.

title: 5  Generation Integration
text:
5.5 Framework-Based Implementations
Frameworks can help with prompt templates, chains, document objects, and response parsing. They speed up prototyping and reduce boilerplate, especially in early development.
The risk is abstraction leakage. Teams should still understand what documents are passed, how prompts are assembled, and where errors originate.

title: 5  Generation Integration
5  Generation Integration
text:
5.7 Function Calling for Tool Integration
Function calling extends RAG beyond passive answering. After retrieval, the model may decide to invoke a tool: fetch a record, create a ticket, run a query, or verify a citation.
This turns the system into an agentic workflow, which can be powerful but also more failure-prone. Tool selection, argument validation, and permission boundaries therefore become part of the RAG design space.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.1 Why Evaluation Matters
By this point in the book, the system can:
That sounds like a complete pipeline. But one hard question remains:
This is where evaluation comes in.
Evaluation is not just about getting one final score. Its real job is to help us see what is working, what is failing, and where the failure starts.
That matters because a weak answer can come from several different places:
If we only look at the final answer, those failure modes get mixed together.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.2 The RAG Evaluation Triad
A useful mental model is the evaluation triad:

context relevance: did the system retrieve the right evidence?
faithfulness or groundedness: did the answer stay supported by that evidence?
answer relevance: did the answer actually address the user’s need?

These dimensions are related but distinct. A system can retrieve relevant context and still generate an off-target answer. It can also answer fluently while being weakly grounded.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.4 Context Relevance
Context relevance is about the retrieved material.
The basic question is:

if a human looked at these retrieved chunks, would they say these are good pieces of evidence for answering the question?

This matters because the generator can only work with what retrieval gives it.
If the retrieved chunks are weak, incomplete, or off-topic, generation is already at a disadvantage.
Some practical signs of poor context relevance are:

the right chapter never appears;
the right topic appears, but only in a vague way;
the top results repeat each other instead of covering the answer;
one crucial chunk is missing.

In practice, inspecting retrieved chunks directly is often one of the fastest ways to improve a system.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.4 Faithfulness and Groundedness
Faithfulness asks whether the answer is supported by the retrieved evidence. Grounded systems should be able to point to where a claim came from, not merely sound convincing.
This is one of the most important properties in high-stakes applications. When faithfulness is weak, polished language becomes a liability rather than a strength.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.6 Answer Relevance
An answer can be grounded and still not be very helpful.
For example:

it may summarize the wrong part of the evidence;
it may answer a nearby question instead of the user’s actual question;
it may be too vague;
it may omit the comparison or decision the user needed.

That is why answer relevance is its own category.
The key question is:

did the answer actually solve the user’s task?

This is important because good retrieval and good grounding are necessary, but not sufficient. The answer still has to be useful.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.6 Evaluation Workflows
A practical evaluation workflow usually includes:

a representative query set;
expected behaviors or reference answers;
per-stage logging for retrieval and generation;
automated metrics where useful;
targeted human review for difficult cases.

Evaluation should be iterative and continuous, not a one-time gate before launch.

6.6.1 A Tiny Local Evaluation Loop
This book includes a deliberately small evaluation script at code/cli/evaluate_book.py. It runs a handful of example questions from code/data/book_eval_questions.json, calls the book-question-answering pipeline, and checks two basic conditions:

whether the expected chapter is retrieved;
whether the final answer contains the expected key ideas.

You can run it with:
python3 code/cli/evaluate_book.py --chat-model qwen3.5:2b --embed-model bge-m3:latest
The script is intentionally lightweight:
for item in items:
    output = run_question(item["question"], chat_model=args.chat_model, embed_model=args.embed_model)
    retrieved_ok = item["expected_source_path"] in output
    answer_ok = all(keyword.lower() in output.lower() for keyword in item["expected_keywords"])
This is not a complete evaluation framework, but it teaches the right habit: measure retrieval and generation separately whenever possible.
In the current project, the evaluation script can be pointed at a selected CLI question-answering configuration. For example, a cloud-model run can be tested with:
python3 code/cli/evaluate_book.py --chat-model deepseek-v3.1:671b-cloud --embed-model bge-m3:latest
The interactive Streamlit app can also use different local or cloud chat models, but those UI runs should still be treated as exploratory comparisons unless the model choices are fixed inside an evaluation workflow.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.7 LlamaIndex Evaluation
Framework-provided evaluation modules can accelerate experimentation by offering ready-made abstractions for query sets, judgments, and comparison runs. They are useful for building a habit of measurement early.
Still, teams should validate that the framework metrics align with product reality rather than treating built-in scores as final truth.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.8 RAGAS
RAGAS popularized a practical approach to assessing RAG behavior across multiple dimensions using model-assisted judgments. It is especially useful when human labeling capacity is limited and relative comparisons matter more than absolute scores.
Like all synthetic or model-based evaluation, it should be triangulated with human inspection.
The tiny evaluator in this book can be seen as the lowest rung on the same ladder. It is not sophisticated, but it forces the system to answer repeatable questions over the real book corpus and gives the reader a place to add stronger metrics later.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.9 Phoenix (Arize Phoenix)
Observability platforms such as Phoenix help teams inspect traces, prompts, retrieved documents, and output quality over time. This is essential once a RAG system serves real users and accumulates operational complexity.
Evaluation is not complete when a metric is computed. It becomes complete when the team can connect measurements back to concrete system changes.
For a learner, the most important step is to start logging and comparing outputs early. Once a local pipeline exists over the real chapter files, even a small JSON evaluation set can reveal regressions after changing chunking, prompts, retrieval weights, or model choices.

title: 7  Advanced RAG Architectures
text:
7.1 When Baseline RAG Stops Being Enough
By this point in the book, we have built up a baseline RAG pipeline:
That baseline is enough for many useful systems. In fact, a lot of teams would benefit more from building a clean baseline well than from jumping too quickly into advanced ideas.
So this chapter is not here to say “basic RAG is obsolete.” It is here to answer a more practical question:
That is the right starting point for advanced architecture.

title: 7  Advanced RAG Architectures
text:
7.2 Knowledge-Graph-Augmented RAG
Knowledge graphs add explicit entities and relationships to the retrieval landscape. Instead of only searching over text similarity, the system can retrieve along graph edges such as ownership, dependency, causality, membership, or chronology.
This is powerful when the meaning of a question lies in how facts connect rather than in whether a paragraph happens to mention them together.

title: 7  Advanced RAG Architectures
7  Advanced RAG Architectures
text:
7.4 Integrating Graph Retrieval with LLMs
When an LLM consumes graph-derived evidence, the prompt or tool layer needs to preserve relationship semantics clearly. A raw dump of nodes and edges is rarely enough. The evidence should be translated into an interpretable form, with provenance and path information when possible.
This makes graph retrieval more understandable to both the model and the human reviewer.

title: 7  Advanced RAG Architectures
8  Summary
text:
RAG is easiest to understand when you stop thinking of it as one trick and start thinking of it as a pipeline. In this book, we walked through that pipeline step by step: prepare the data, build an index, retrieve evidence, generate an answer, and then evaluate what happened.
Several themes repeat throughout every chapter.
First, data quality determines system quality. No embedding model, vector database, or prompt can fully compensate for broken ingestion, poor chunk boundaries, stale documents, or inconsistent metadata.
Second, retrieval is a modeling problem, not just an infrastructure problem. The act of converting a user request into evidence selection requires choices about representation, indexing, query formulation, ranking, filtering, and context assembly.
Third, generation must be treated as a constrained interface. Once evidence is retrieved, the system still needs careful prompt design, output structure, and tool orchestration to produce answers that are useful and grounded.
Fourth, evaluation cannot be postponed. A RAG system that looks good in demos may still fail in coverage, grounding, latency, or operational robustness. Strong teams define quality criteria early and measure them continuously.
Finally, more advanced RAG systems do not win by being more complicated for its own sake. They win when the retrieval design actually matches the problem. Sometimes chunk retrieval is enough. Sometimes the task needs metadata filters, SQL, graph traversal, or tool use.
If this book has one practical message, it is this: build RAG systems layer by layer, inspect what they are doing, and improve them with evidence rather than guesswork.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
raw chapters
-> cleaned and chunked text
-> embeddings for each chunk
-> searchable index
-> retrieval at question time

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.2 What This Chapter Is Trying to Solve

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.3 Why Plain Text Search Is Not Enough
text:
3.3 Why Plain Text Search Is Not Enough
Suppose a chunk says:
RAG is strongest when the problem is access to external knowledge.
A user might ask:
When should I use RAG instead of fine-tuning?
The wording is different, but the meaning is related. Simple keyword search may do fine in some cases, but it can also miss useful matches when the query and the chunk use different words.
That is why dense retrieval exists. Instead of matching only on surface words, it tries to match on semantic similarity.
This does not mean keyword methods stop mattering. It means we often need another tool in addition to exact term matching.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.4 Embeddings and Their Role in RAG

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.5 What an Embedding Model Actually Helps With

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.6 How This Project Builds an Index

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.7 Why the Current Index Is Simple but Useful

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.8 Dense Retrieval in Plain Language
Once the chunks are embedded, the system can compare a question embedding with each stored chunk embedding.
In this project, that comparison is done with cosine similarity:
def cosine_similarity(vec_a: list[float], vec_b: list[float]) -> float:
    numerator = sum(a * b for a, b in zip(vec_a, vec_b))
    norm_a = math.sqrt(sum(a * a for a in vec_a))
    norm_b = math.sqrt(sum(b * b for b in vec_b))
    if norm_a == 0 or norm_b == 0:
        return 0.0
    return numerator / (norm_a * norm_b)
You do not need to worry too much about the math here. The practical meaning is enough: the closer the vectors point in the same direction, the more similar the system considers the texts.
That gives the system a way to rank chunks by semantic closeness to the user’s question.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.9 Why Embeddings Alone Are Not the Whole Story

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.10 How to Choose an Embedding Model

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.11 The Difference Between an Embedding, an Index, and a Vector Database
text:
3.11 The Difference Between an Embedding, an Index, and a Vector Database
These terms are easy to blur together, so it is worth separating them clearly.
An embedding model:

turns text into vectors.

An index:

is the searchable data structure built from those vectors.

A vector database:

is a storage and retrieval system built to manage vector indexes, metadata, updates, and search at larger scale.

In a small project, these can all feel like one thing because the same script may handle all of them. But they are different layers.
This repository has:

an embedding model;
a simple local JSON index;
no full vector database yet.

That distinction matters because it helps you see what problem each tool is solving.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.12 Why ANN Search Exists
If a project has only a small number of chunks, it is possible to compare a question embedding against every chunk directly. That is basically what this project does now.
But as the corpus grows, that gets slower and more expensive.
Approximate nearest-neighbor search, usually shortened to ANN, is the family of methods used to make vector search faster at larger scale. The core tradeoff is simple:

exact search tries harder to find the very best matches;
approximate search is faster, but may miss some of them.

This tradeoff is normal. The point is not to be mathematically perfect. The point is to meet the application’s quality and latency needs.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.14 Vector Databases and When They Matter

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
3  Index Construction and Optimization
text:
3.16 Context Expansion After Retrieval
Sometimes the best-matching chunk is still too narrow to answer the question well.
For example, the chunk may contain a definition, but not the caveat that appears in the next paragraph. In that case, the system may benefit from adding neighboring chunks or the parent section.
This is usually called context expansion.
It can help because:

answers often need a little more surrounding context;
documents often contain meaning spread across nearby chunks.

It can also hurt because:

more context means more tokens;
extra context can add noise.

So this is another design choice that should be tested, not assumed.

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.17 How to Tell Whether Indexing Is Working

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.18 What This Chapter Adds to the Pipeline
text:
3.18 What This Chapter Adds to the Pipeline
Chapter 2 produced retrieval-ready chunks.
Chapter 3 turns those chunks into a searchable index.
So the handoff now becomes:
documents
-> cleaned chunks with metadata
-> embeddings
-> index
-> retrieval
That sets up the next chapter naturally. Once the system has chunks and a way to search them, the next question is no longer “How do we store information?” It becomes “How do we retrieve the right information reliably?”

title: 1  Introduction to RAG
1  Introduction to RAG
section: 1.2 What Problem RAG Is Trying to Solve
text:
1.2 What Problem RAG Is Trying to Solve
The basic problem is simple.
Language models are very good at producing fluent text, but they are not a reliable place to store all the facts a real application may need. Product details change. Internal documents change. Policies change. Some information should never have been in public training data to begin with.
So the practical question becomes:

how do we give a model access to the right external information when a user asks?

RAG is one answer to that question.
It does not replace the model. It gives the model evidence to work from.
That is why RAG is often a better fit than plain prompting when the answer needs to depend on real source material instead of only the model’s prior knowledge.

title: 1  Introduction to RAG
1  Introduction to RAG
section: 1.9 What This Chapter Adds to the Pipeline
text:
1.9 What This Chapter Adds to the Pipeline
This chapter gives us the starting mental model for the rest of the book:
question
-> retrieve evidence
-> answer from that evidence
The next chapter takes the first real build step in that pipeline:

where does that evidence come from, and how do we prepare it well enough for retrieval?

title: 3  Index Construction and Optimization
section: 3.17 How to Tell Whether the Index Is Working

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
4  Retrieval Optimization
section: 4.2 What Retrieval Is Really Trying to Do
text:
4.2 What Retrieval Is Really Trying to Do
In plain language, retrieval is trying to do two things at once:

not miss useful evidence;
not include too much irrelevant evidence.

Those goals pull in opposite directions.
If retrieval is too broad, the generator gets noisy context and may answer vaguely. If retrieval is too narrow, the generator may miss the key evidence and answer from partial information.
That is why retrieval work is mostly about tradeoffs, not perfect rules.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.3 Dense Retrieval
Dense retrieval uses embeddings for both the question and the stored chunks. It is strong when the user’s wording and the document’s wording are different, but the meaning is similar.
For example, a user might ask:
When is RAG a better choice than fine-tuning?
The relevant chunk might say:
RAG is strongest when the challenge is access to external knowledge.
The exact words do not match perfectly, but the meaning is close. Dense retrieval is good at this kind of match.
In this project, dense retrieval works by embedding the question and comparing it with each chunk embedding using cosine similarity.
The practical strength of dense retrieval is semantic matching.
The practical weakness is that semantic matching is not always enough.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
section: 4.5 Dense and Sparse Fail in Different Ways

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
4  Retrieval Optimization
section: 4.7 A Simple Example: Why Hybrid Can Help
text:
4.7 A Simple Example: Why Hybrid Can Help
Imagine a user asks:
How does the book compare RAG with fine-tuning?
One chunk may contain the exact phrase “RAG vs. Fine-Tuning.” Another may talk about “external knowledge” and “behavior” without repeating the full query wording.
Sparse retrieval may prefer the chunk with the exact phrase.
Dense retrieval may prefer the chunk with the strongest semantic explanation.
Hybrid retrieval has a chance to surface both kinds of evidence near the top.
That often makes the final answer better because the generator sees both the direct match and the more explanatory support.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
section: 4.8 Retrieval as a Pipeline, Not One Step

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.10 Query Rewriting and Query Construction
Users do not always ask clean, retrieval-friendly questions.
A user may be vague:
What does the book say about when to use this?
Or too broad:
Explain retrieval.
Or too conversational:
I kind of forget, what's the part where dense and sparse are combined?
Query rewriting tries to turn that into a better retrieval query.
This can include:

adding likely keywords;
rewriting into a clearer form;
splitting one broad question into several narrower ones.

The benefit is obvious: better retrieval inputs can lead to better results.
The danger is also obvious: rewriting should not change what the user meant.
So query rewriting is helpful, but it should be treated carefully.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.11 Re-ranking
A common pattern in retrieval is that the system finds a decent candidate set, but the order is not great.
For example, maybe the best chunk is in the top 10, but not in the top 3. If the generator only sees the top few chunks, that ordering problem becomes an answer-quality problem.
Re-ranking is a second pass over a smaller candidate set. A stronger relevance model looks at those candidates and tries to sort them better.
This is useful when:

first-stage retrieval has decent recall;
but the final ordering is noisy.

In a small project, it is often smart to understand dense, sparse, and hybrid retrieval first. Only after that should reranking be added. Otherwise it becomes hard to tell where the improvement came from.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.12 Context Assembly
Even after ranking, the system still has one more important job: deciding what context to actually pass to the generator.
This is not trivial.
If the system passes only one narrow chunk, the answer may miss key detail.
If it passes too many chunks, the prompt may become noisy.
So context assembly asks questions like:

how many chunks should be included;
should neighboring chunks be added;
should duplicate or near-duplicate chunks be removed;
should very long chunks be trimmed.

This is where retrieval quality and generation quality meet.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.13 Context Expansion and Compression
Two opposite moves are common here.
Context expansion means adding nearby or related text when one chunk is too narrow on its own.
Context compression means trimming or summarizing when the retrieved material is too long or repetitive.
Both are trying to solve the same problem:

give the generator enough evidence, but not too much noise.

Neither move is always correct. Both should be tested on real questions.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
4  Retrieval Optimization
text:
4.15 How to Inspect Retrieval Failures
When retrieval feels weak, it helps to ask a few concrete questions.
Did the right chunk never appear at all?

Then the problem may be chunking, indexing, metadata, or query mismatch.

Did the right chunk appear, but too low in the ranking?

Then the problem may be scoring or the lack of reranking.

Did several top chunks say almost the same thing?

Then the problem may be duplication or weak context assembly.

Did retrieval return something related but not specific enough?

Then dense retrieval may be too broad on its own, or the query may need better construction.

This kind of debugging matters because “retrieval is bad” is too vague to fix.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
section: 4.16 How This Project Lets You Compare Retrieval Methods
text:
4.16 How This Project Lets You Compare Retrieval Methods
This repository includes code/cli/retrieval_compare.py, which is helpful because it makes retrieval visible.
You can run:
python3 code/cli/ingest_book.py
python3 code/cli/build_book_index.py --embed-model bge-m3:latest
python3 code/cli/retrieval_compare.py --question "How does the book compare RAG with fine-tuning?" --embed-model bge-m3:latest
That script compares:

dense retrieval;
sparse retrieval;
hybrid retrieval.

This is useful because it trains the right habit: inspect the retrieved chunks directly. Do not judge the system only by the final answer. Often the fastest way to improve a RAG system is to look at what was retrieved and ask whether that evidence actually makes sense.

title: 4  Retrieval Optimization
section: 4.17 What This Chapter Adds to the Pipeline
text:
4.17 What This Chapter Adds to the Pipeline
At this point, the project flow looks like this:
documents
-> cleaned chunks
-> embeddings and index
-> retrieval and ranking
-> prompt context
-> answer
So the next step is clear.
Once retrieval has selected evidence, the system still needs to turn that evidence into a good prompt and a good answer. That is the job of the next chapter.

title: 5  Generation Integration
5  Generation Integration
section: 5.2 What Often Goes Wrong at This Stage

title: 5  Generation Integration
section: 5.3 What the Generation Layer Is Trying to Do
text:
5.3 What the Generation Layer Is Trying to Do
In plain language, the generation layer is trying to do three things well:

use the retrieved evidence;
stay inside what the evidence supports;
present the answer in a useful form.

That sounds straightforward, but each part has tradeoffs.
If the instructions are too weak, the model may ignore the evidence.
If the instructions are too rigid, the answer may become awkward or incomplete.
If too much evidence is passed, the model may lose focus.
If too little evidence is passed, the model may miss an important detail.
So generation is not just “ask the model nicely.” It is the stage where retrieval output becomes a usable response.

title: 5  Generation Integration
text:
5.5 Why Prompting Matters So Much
Without a clear prompt, the model may treat retrieved evidence as optional background instead of required evidence.
For example, if the prompt simply says:
Answer this question: What is hybrid retrieval?
the model may produce a generic answer from its own prior knowledge.
If the prompt instead makes the grounding rule explicit, the model is more likely to use the retrieved evidence instead of treating it as decoration.
This is why prompt design in RAG is not just about style. It is about control.

title: 5  Generation Integration
text:
5.6 Grounded Answers vs. Plausible Answers
This is one of the most important ideas in the whole book.
A plausible answer is an answer that sounds right.
A grounded answer is an answer that is actually supported by the retrieved evidence.
Those are not the same thing.
A large language model is very good at producing plausible language. RAG is supposed to make that language more grounded. If the generation layer does not enforce grounding, the system can sound smart while still drifting away from the source.
So a good generation setup should make unsupported answering harder, not easier.

title: 5  Generation Integration
section: 5.7 What to Do When the Evidence Is Weak
text:
5.7 What to Do When the Evidence Is Weak
Not every question will have enough support in the retrieved chunks.
When that happens, the system should not confidently improvise.
Better behaviors include:

clearly saying the retrieved evidence is insufficient;
answering only the supported part;
asking for clarification in interactive systems;
returning citations that show what was actually found.

This is why the current prompt explicitly says:

if the retrieved evidence is insufficient, say that clearly.

That single instruction matters more than it may seem. It gives the model permission to be incomplete instead of forcing it to fake completeness.

title: 5  Generation Integration
5  Generation Integration
section: 5.9 The Shape of the Final Answer

title: 5  Generation Integration
text:
5.10 Structured Output
Structured output means telling the model to respond in a format with clear fields.
This is useful when the answer needs to be:

checked by code;
shown in a predictable UI;
compared during evaluation;
passed to another tool or workflow.

For example, a support workflow may want:

answer
confidence
sources
follow_up_needed

instead of a single free-form paragraph.
The main benefit is control. The main risk is brittleness if the model does not follow the format well.
That is why structured output usually works best when the task is narrow and the expected shape is clear.

title: 5  Generation Integration
5  Generation Integration
section: 5.12 Frameworks Can Help, but They Do Not Replace Understanding

title: 5  Generation Integration
5  Generation Integration
text:
5.14 How to Inspect Generation Failures
When the final answer feels wrong, it helps to ask a few concrete questions.
Did retrieval return the right evidence?

If not, the problem is upstream.

Did retrieval return good evidence, but the answer ignored it?

Then the prompt or context assembly may be weak.

Did the answer add claims that were not in the evidence?

Then the grounding rule may be too weak.

Did the answer include the right facts, but in a messy or unhelpful format?

Then output design may need work.

This kind of debugging matters because “the model answered badly” is usually too vague to fix.

title: 5  Generation Integration
section: 5.15 What This Chapter Adds to the Pipeline
text:
5.15 What This Chapter Adds to the Pipeline
At this point, the project flow looks like this:
documents
-> cleaned chunks
-> embeddings and index
-> retrieval and ranking
-> prompt assembly
-> grounded answer
So the next chapter can ask a new question:

how do we know whether this whole system is actually working well?

That is the job of evaluation.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
6  RAG System Evaluation
section: 6.2 Why RAG Evaluation Is Harder Than Plain LLM Evaluation
text:
6.2 Why RAG Evaluation Is Harder Than Plain LLM Evaluation
A plain LLM task might only ask:

did the model answer well?

A RAG task has to ask at least two more things:

did the system retrieve the right evidence;
did the answer stay supported by that evidence.

So RAG evaluation is harder because it is not judging one step. It is judging a chain of steps.
That is why a final answer can be bad even when the retriever did a good job. The opposite can also happen: the answer may sound fine even though retrieval was weak.
This is the core reason evaluation needs to be broken into parts.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.3 The Three Main Questions
A simple way to think about RAG evaluation is to ask three questions.
First:

did the system retrieve useful evidence?

Second:

did the answer stay supported by that evidence?

Third:

did the answer actually help with the user’s question?

These three questions are often described as:

context relevance;
grounding, also called faithfulness;
answer relevance.

The names are less important than the idea behind them.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.5 Grounding
Grounding asks whether the final answer is supported by the retrieved evidence.
In plainer language:

did the answer stick to what the retrieved text actually supports?

This matters because a model can sound convincing while still adding claims that were not in the evidence.
That is a dangerous failure mode in RAG. The whole reason to retrieve evidence is to reduce unsupported answering. If the answer drifts away from the evidence, the system loses much of its value.
Some practical signs of weak grounding are:

the answer includes details that never appeared in the retrieved chunks;
the answer overstates what the evidence actually said;
the answer merges evidence and guesswork without making the difference clear;
the citation line looks plausible, but the cited chunks do not really support the claim.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
section: 6.7 A Simple Way to Debug with These Three Questions
text:
6.7 A Simple Way to Debug with These Three Questions
These three checks are useful because they help locate the problem.
If context relevance is weak:

the problem is likely in data preparation, indexing, retrieval, or filtering.

If context relevance is good, but grounding is weak:

the problem is likely in prompt design, context assembly, or generation behavior.

If context relevance and grounding are both decent, but answer relevance is weak:

the problem may be task framing, output design, or the way the question is interpreted.

This is why evaluation is not just measurement. It is diagnosis.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
section: 6.8 What a Practical Evaluation Workflow Looks Like

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
section: 6.9 The Small Evaluation Loop in This Project
text:
6.9 The Small Evaluation Loop in This Project
This repository already includes a small evaluation script:

code/cli/evaluate_book.py

It uses a small question set from:

code/data/book_eval_questions.json

The script runs the question-answering pipeline and checks two basic things:

whether the expected chapter appears in the output;
whether the answer includes expected keywords.

You can run it like this:
python3 code/cli/evaluate_book.py --chat-model qwen3.5:2b --embed-model bge-m3:latest
The core loop is simple:
for item in items:
    output = run_question(item["question"], chat_model=args.chat_model, embed_model=args.embed_model)
    retrieved_ok = item["expected_source_path"] in output
    answer_ok = all(keyword.lower() in output.lower() for keyword in item["expected_keywords"])
This is not a full evaluation framework, but it already teaches the right habit:

ask repeatable questions;
check retrieval behavior;
check answer behavior;
compare results after changes.

For a learning project, that is a good start.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
6  RAG System Evaluation
section: 6.11 What This Evaluation Does Not Yet Do

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
6  RAG System Evaluation
text:
6.13 Model-Based Evaluation and Framework Tools
As systems grow, teams often add stronger evaluation tools.
These may include:

model-based judges;
frameworks such as RAGAS;
observability tools such as Phoenix;
richer trace logging and comparison dashboards.

These tools can be useful, especially when the system becomes too large for only manual review.
But they should be treated as aids, not as magic truth machines.
The important question is always the same:

do these evaluation methods help us understand and improve real system behavior?

If the answer is yes, they are useful. If not, they can become another layer of noise.

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
section: 6.14 What to Measure as the Project Grows

title: 6  RAG System Evaluation
section: 6.15 What This Chapter Adds to the Pipeline
text:
6.15 What This Chapter Adds to the Pipeline
At this point, the project flow looks like this:
documents
-> cleaned chunks
-> embeddings and index
-> retrieval and ranking
-> prompt assembly
-> answer
-> evaluation
That gives the project a full loop.
The next chapter can now step back and ask a broader question:

once the baseline RAG pipeline is working, what does it look like to go beyond this basic design?

That is where advanced architectures come in.

title: 7  Advanced RAG Architectures
7  Advanced RAG Architectures
section: 7.2 The Limits of a Simple Retrieve-Then-Generate Loop

title: 7  Advanced RAG Architectures
text:
7.3 Multi-Hop Questions
One common limit of baseline RAG is the multi-hop question.
A multi-hop question is one where the answer depends on combining more than one piece of evidence.
For example:

which component depends on the service owned by team X;
what changed between the earlier policy and the later version;
which retrieval method does the book recommend, and why.

A baseline retriever may find one useful chunk, but miss the second or third piece needed to complete the answer.
This does not mean baseline RAG is useless. It means retrieval and context assembly may need to become more deliberate.

title: 7  Advanced RAG Architectures
7  Advanced RAG Architectures
7  Advanced RAG Architectures
7  Advanced RAG Architectures
7  Advanced RAG Architectures
7  Advanced RAG Architectures
7  Advanced RAG Architectures
7  Advanced RAG Architectures
section: 7.11 Observability Becomes More Important as Architecture Grows

title: 7  Advanced RAG Architectures
section: 7.12 Do Not Upgrade Architecture Too Early
text:
7.12 Do Not Upgrade Architecture Too Early
This may be the most important advice in the chapter.
It is easy to read about graph RAG, agentic retrieval, or multi-backend routing and assume that a stronger system must be more complex.
That is not always true.
A weak baseline does not become strong by adding architectural layers. If chunking is poor, retrieval is weak, or evaluation is shallow, advanced architecture may only make the system harder to understand.
So the better rule is:

move to advanced architecture when the current design has a clear limit you can name.

That limit might be:

too many multi-hop failures;
too many structured queries;
too much need for metadata filtering;
too much dependence on live records;
too many question types for one retriever to handle well.

If you cannot name the current limit, the system may not be ready for a bigger architecture.

title: 7  Advanced RAG Architectures
section: 7.13 What This Means for This Project

title: 7  Advanced RAG Architectures
text:
7.14 Closing Thought
The main lesson of advanced RAG is not that every system should become more complicated.
The real lesson is that retrieval should match the shape of the problem.
Sometimes a clean chunk-and-retrieve pipeline is enough.
Sometimes the task requires multiple retrieval methods, live systems, or explicit relationships.
The important skill is not memorizing architecture names. It is learning to see what kind of evidence a question really needs, and then designing the retrieval system around that need.
